Vintage Computer FestivalVCF West 2026live demo & talk
An AI writes machine code for a 4-bit computer from 1981

VIBE-CODING A
1981 MICROCOMPUTER

Claude (Anthropic) hand-writes Microtronic machine code — games, OLED graphics and speech — for the Busch Microtronic 2090 + PicoRAM 2090, and debugs every one on real hardware. Six new programs, only existing op-codes, no firmware changes.
A first: the first working Tic-Tac-Toe for the Busch Microtronic — ever. The 1981 manual’s own listing had an address typo and was never correctly run; we re-derived and verified the authentic 58-word strategy.
The showcase two-bank Tic-Tac-Toe is the first PicoRAM program to combine OLED graphics + speech + SRAM bank-switching + a real game AI all at once. (PicoRAM 2090 won the RetroChallenge 2023/10 Grand Prize.)

01THE MACHINE

Busch Microtronic 2090 (1981, West Germany) — an educational microcomputer, a “learn how a computer works” trainer. A TI TMS1600 4-bit microcontroller (~500 kHz), 16 four-bit registers (R0–RF), 256 program words per bank, a hex keypad and a 6-digit red LED. You key in programs one 3-hex-digit word at a time, by hand; the monitor lives in a masked ROM — physically unchangeable.

PicoRAM 2090 (Michael Wessel, 2023) — a Raspberry Pi Pico in the Microtronic’s 2114 SRAM sockets: it emulates the RAM and adds SD-card load/save of memory dumps — plus I/O the 1981 machine never had: a 128×32 OLED, a speech synthesizer, sound, and a battery-buffered real-time clock.

02SEEN IN ACTION

Why it’s hard: no compiler, no on-machine assembler, no screen editor — just 256 words of 4-bit memory and a keypad. Every byte of game logic, board graphics, AI and speech is placed by hand within that budget.

▶  Try it at the table! The Microtronic + PicoRAM is running right here — beat the Tic-Tac-Toe AI, land the Eagle, or solve MERLIN.

03THE TRICK — NEW INSTRUCTIONS FOR A SEALED MACHINE

The Microtronic’s firmware ROM is mask-programmed into the TMS1600 chip itself — there is no external ROM/EPROM to swap, and the chip cannot be reflashed. It is doubly sealed, so PicoRAM can’t add real CPU instructions. Instead it watches the data bus for op-codes that never occur in normal programs — “vacuous” no-ops that change nothing — and repurposes them as new side effects:

50F = “add 0 to register F” → changes nothing → PicoRAM hears: SPEAK
0xx = “move register x to itself” → a no-op → PicoRAM reads it as an argument / GRAPHICS

The 4-bit CPU runs them as if nothing happened; the Pico gives them meaning — a whole graphics-and-speech API smuggled into the gaps of a 45-year-old instruction set (502 clear · 504 refresh · 506 char · 508 cursor · 50A line · 50F speak) — plus 70x to switch SRAM banks and break the 256-word ceiling.

04THE PROGRAMS — SIX NEW GAMES

  • TIC-TAC-TOE ×2 — the first working tic-tac-toe for the Microtronic, ever. Human-first (two-bank) — you move first against a board-scanning tactical AI (win→block→center→corner→side): across all 489 lines of play it wins 350, draws 127, beatable only by a deliberate two-corner fork. AI in bank 0, OLED + speech in bank 1. Computer-first — the authentic Busch 58-word look-ahead, verbatim, with OLED + speech (“YOU?” / “I WIN” / “DRAW”).
  • MERLIN + MERLIN 2 — graphical “Lights Out”: turn all nine OLED cells on; key beeps + a victory melody. MERLIN 2 adds difficulty — set a move budget and beat the move counter, or get a sad falling D-minor melody and a new puzzle.
  • BLOCKADE — the two-lane Nim game from the Busch 2094 “Computerspiele” booklet, logic (00–87) transcribed word-for-word from the manual, given an OLED makeover + “YOU WIN”/“I WIN” speech; flicker-free incremental redraw, exactly 256 words.
  • ★ LUNAR LANDER — the odd one out: not a port — a wholly original game designed from scratch by the AI. Turn-based (the 1969 form). Red LED = ALT·VEL·FUEL; OLED = the window (lander, thrust flame, smoking crater); land at velocity ≤ 2 for “THE EAGLE HAS LANDED”, else “CRASH”. Pure-integer physics, 172 words.
    descending burning landed crashed A A V ________ ________ ___A___ __*X*__
▶  Watch it built — live.
A YouTube stream of Claude vibe-coding the Microtronic on real 1981 hardware.
youtu.be/Smc7S6Ny78w

05HOW CLAUDE BUILT IT

  • Hand-wrote raw Microtronic machine code — three hex digits per word, only existing op-codes, no firmware changes.
  • Wrote an assembler, masm.py — readable .asm (labels, mnemonics, raw words) → loadable .MIC.
  • Wrote the headline tool, mtsim.py — a two-bank simulator that renders the OLED as text and transcripts the speech, so an entire game is played and verified off-device before touching hardware.
  • Modeled the quirks that actually bite: no-borrow compare carry, the 70x bank-switch prefetch (handoff = 70x; NOP; GOTO), and the 50x argument stream (stray no-op words get swallowed as draw/speech args).
  • Verified in the simulator, then on a real Microtronic — iterating with Michael over photos of the live hardware.
  • Learned the machine from its 1981 German manual (which we translated to English); the extended op-codes are undocumented, so Claude read the PicoRAM firmware source.
Programs by Claude (Anthropic) · with Michael Wessel, on real hardware · code & write-ups: github.com/lambdamikel/picoram2090 → software/vibe-coded · Busch Microtronic © Busch 1981, PicoRAM © M. Wessel 2023
